mnemonics: COUNT THE RATIONALS, MEASURE THE REALS: A set is countable (like the rationals, Q) if you can 'count' its elements, even if it's infinite. A set is uncountable (like the reals, R) if it's too vast to count and its 'size' (measure) is fundamentally larger.
keyPoints: A set is countable if its elements can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers (N).
A set is uncountable if it is infinite but cannot be put into a one-to-one correspondence with N. The real numbers (R) are the classic example.
The cardinality of a countable set is ℵ₀ (aleph-null). The cardinality of the continuum (e.g., R) is a larger cardinality, often denoted c.
commonMistakes: Believing all infinite sets have the same size. Countable and uncountable infinities are not the same; |R| > |N|.
Assuming a set is uncountable just because it is infinite. Many infinite sets, like the integers (Z) or rationals (Q), are countable.
Confusing the concepts of 'dense' and 'uncountable'. Q is dense in R but is countable, while R is both dense and uncountable.
